title: Draught-sealing recessed downlights in an insulated ceiling
subtitle: Fire-rated covers and perimeter sealing for loft-side access
excerpt: Every recessed downlight is a hole through the ceiling where warm air leaks directly into the loft. A fire-rated cover sealed with foam and tape costs around 3-5 pounds per fitting and takes under ten minutes each. For a kitchen with twelve downlights, the draught saving alone exceeds 150 kWh per year.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B (2022) on airtightness requirements for existing dwellings. Energy Saving Trust downlight guidance. BS 476-3 and BS EN 1366-3 fire resistance standards referenced for cover product selection.

glossaryTerms.definition: A localised path of relatively high thermal conductance through an otherwise well-insulated building envelope, causing a cold spot, surface condensation risk, and elevated heat loss.

content.text: Recessed downlights are one of the worst airtightness failures in a modern insulated ceiling. Each fitting sits in a hole 70-100 mm in diameter with a small gap around the lamp body. Warm, moist air from the kitchen or bathroom rises through that gap directly into the loft, bypassing whatever insulation sits above. Each unsealed fitting also acts as a
thermal bridge
through the ceiling plane. For a kitchen with twelve downlights, Building Research Establishment data suggests 150-250 kWh per year in heat lost through those holes alone.

The standard fix is a fire-rated insulation cover, sometimes called a 'fire hood' or 'downlight cover', installed from above in the loft or ceiling void. The cover sits over each fitting, sealed to the ceiling joist or plasterboard with foam. Material cost per fitting is 3-5 pounds. For twelve fittings, the job takes two to three hours including access. Payback at 2025/26 UK electricity rates is inside twelve months.

Before climbing into the loft, isolate the lighting circuit at the consumer unit. Test the circuit with a known-good tester before touching any fitting. The covers must be fire-rated: confirm each product carries a BS 476-3 or BS EN 1366-3 rating. Non-rated polystyrene boxes are not acceptable above fire-risk rooms.
What you need to know first

Access: the covers are installed from above, either through the loft hatch or through the ceiling void between floors. Confirm access before buying covers; some ceiling voids are accessible only by lifting flooring.
Lamp type: fire-rated covers have a maximum wattage limit, typically 35 W for incandescent or halogen equivalents. LED lamps run much cooler (5-8 W) and are compatible with all fire-rated covers.

Count the fittings and measure the clearance height needed. Most downlight covers are 165-170 mm tall; check that the loft floor or ceiling void has enough depth to accommodate them without compressing existing insulation.
Materials
Fire-rated downlight covers: one per fitting. Polypropylene or vermiculite-coated steel products rated to BS 476-3 or BS EN 1366-3.
Low-expansion polyurethane foam: one 500 ml can covers eight to ten fittings.
Aluminium foil tape, 50 mm: for reinforcing the perimeter seal where the cover meets the plasterboard.
Installation sequence
Isolate the circuit at the consumer unit. Test the circuit dead before entering the loft.
In the loft or ceiling void, locate each downlight. Clear any insulation from the area around each fitting so the cover base sits flat on the plasterboard.
Place the cover over the downlight body. The cover base should sit on the upper face of the plasterboard with a 5-10 mm clearance ring around the light fitting.

Run a continuous bead of low-expansion foam around the base perimeter of the cover, where it meets the plasterboard. Use the foam sparingly; high-expansion foam can bow the plasterboard. Allow 10 minutes before handling.
After the foam has tacked, press aluminium foil tape over the foam bead to reinforce the seal. Lap 25 mm onto both the cover base and the plasterboard surface.
Replace the insulation around and over each cover. The insulation can contact the cover directly without fire risk; the cover is rated for direct insulation contact.
Restore power and test each fitting before sealing the loft hatch.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: every cover base is sealed to the plasterboard with no visible gap, the tape reinforcement laps both surfaces fully, and the insulation is restored to its full depth over the covers. The room below shows no draught from the fittings when tested with a hand on a cold day.

Done badly: covers placed loosely with no foam or tape, gaps left at the edges, or insulation stuffed into the open cover (a fire risk). Non-fire-rated boxes protect nothing and melt if a fitting overheats.

attrs.symptom: Cover does not sit flat on the plasterboard
attrs.cause: Wiring loops or connector blocks protrude above the plasterboard level
attrs.fix: Press the wiring to one side inside the void and confirm the cable routes do not prevent the cover sitting flush. If a connector block is too large, reposition it outside the cover footprint.

attrs.symptom: Fitting runs warm after cover is installed
attrs.cause: Cover is too small, restricting ventilation around the lamp body
attrs.fix: Replace with a larger-diameter cover or a cover with ventilation slots rated for LED loads. LED fittings at 5-8 W rarely overheat inside correctly sized covers.

attrs.symptom: Draught still apparent from fitting after sealing
attrs.cause: Foam bead is incomplete, or the wiring entry hole in the plasterboard was not sealed before the cover was placed
attrs.fix: Lift the cover, apply foam to the cable entry hole, replace and reseal the perimeter.
